title: First real client
description:
I had edited for a lot of companies. But never directly. Always through someone else.

One day, I finally felt ready to find my own clients. No portfolio — because everything I had made belonged to my employers. I didn't have the right to share most of it.
author: Amin

blocks.content:
I had edited for a lot of companies. But never directly. Always through someone else.

One day, I finally felt ready to find my own clients. No portfolio — because everything I had made belonged to my employers. I didn't have the right to share most of it.

So I built a rough resume from whatever I could. Started cold messaging businesses. Offered a free short-form video so they could see my work. Told them: "If this isn't for you, at least tell someone it might help."

Nothing happened. Or if it did, people wanted to start but had no content to give me.

Then one day, on my way to the hospital, I saw a message: "I'd like to have a meeting."

I couldn't believe it. The person was a real CEO. I spent my entire hospital shift thinking — is this it? Did I finally do it? Can I start my company with this client?

## The Mistake

I got too excited. I wanted to impress him. I made an unsolicited logo animation for his brand, posted it on LinkedIn, and tagged him two nights before our meeting.

He canceled the meeting. Said it was unprofessional. But in the comments, he said he appreciated the effort.

I asked myself — did my own hard work just ruin my future? I apologized. He kept the meeting. I deleted the post.

## The Meeting

It went well. Mostly about my resume and what he needed. But I didn't understand the workload. I priced myself way too low just to land the project.

A second meeting happened. Everything felt good. Like I had finally made it.

I sent a contract. But I'd never done this before. I don't think he even looked at it.

## The Slow Death

Gaps between meetings were long. It took almost two months after the first meeting to actually start the real work.

Then came the internet cut.

Nationwide unrest. Then a complete blackout — no calls, no texts, no internet for two weeks.

When they finally brought back a weak connection, I could only get online at 4 AM. I had 40GB of footage to download. Speed: 1MB per minute.

Five nights without sleep. Just downloading.

## The Hidden Work

I had 10 days to edit 10 short videos. While also going to the hospital.

Then I discovered what they actually wanted wasn't what they told me. Two formats (vertical and horizontal). Subtitles that had to be re-synced. Fonts. Lower thirds. Guest names and titles — I had to find those myself.

I didn't want to be late. I didn't want to lose this chance. So I pushed harder.

## Getting Worried

By the time I delivered, I had done 3 rounds of revisions. No reference was ever given to me. He never accepted my LinkedIn connection. Never looked at the contract. And I had made 14 short shorts (in 2 formats) without getting paid anything.

## The Payment Problem

In the next meeting, I asked for payment. He was ready to pay. But my biggest fear came true — we were in Iran. Sanctions. No way to transfer money.

I prepared a long text explaining how we could still work together and that there wasn't anything wrong with it. He said he'd look into it.

A few days I was so worried he would change his mind. Eventually, he said the transfer would happen. I celebrated my first real paycheck.

## The Second Cut

I started the next batch of 10 videos. Finished them. Was about to discuss the real price.

Then war escalated. The internet was cut again. Completely.

I had sent the videos, but they needed revisions. By the time he replied, the internet was gone. I couldn't deliver. I spent the first week desperately trying to connect, but in the end, I gave up. One week. One month. Three months. And when the internet finally came back, it was such bad quality that more than half the people couldn't even connect.

![](/images/unrest.jpg)

## The Reframe

I thought I had lost the opportunity that could have gotten me out of this country.

But looking back — I don't see a failure.

I see someone who:

- Worked with a great team
- Worked 5 nights without sleep to deliver
- Did 14 free videos just to prove himself
- Survived two complete internet shutdowns
- Still didn't give up
